perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Interim Period" means a period of four (4) years commencing from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Interim Period" means a period of four (4) years commencing from the Effective Date of this Agreement, but may be extended indefinitely at the sole discretion of THC.
perturbation.explanation: By adding that the 'Interim Period' can be extended indefinitely at the sole discretion of THC introduces uncertainty and contradicts the original fixed term, which could affect the rights and obligations tied to this period.
perturbation.law_citation: UCC § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces an indefinite term at the discretion of one party, potentially violating the UCC's requirement for good faith and commercial reasonableness in contracts with indefinite durations. The added clause makes the term length uncertain, which deviates from standard contract law principles that prefer definiteness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an indefinite extension to the 'Interim Period' at THC's discretion, directly contradicting the original agreement's fixed four-year term. This undermines the definiteness expected in contract law, as explained by the LLM Law Explanation, and can be seen as an indefinite duration. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-309, addresses contracts with indefinite durations and implies they are valid for a reasonable time, but the modification gives THC sole discretion for an indefinite extension, potentially exceeding a reasonable time and lacking good faith, particularly impacting obligations tied to that timeframe. The added clause removes the certainty of the interim period, creating a contradiction based on Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Insurance. HERC shall, throughout the term of this Agreement, obtain and maintain at its own cost and expense, from a qualified AAA-rated insurance company, a standard liability insurance and business interruption policy along with advertising injury 7 protection, all of which must be acceptable to THC and HSI, and which must name THC and HSI as additional insureds. Such policy shall provide, in addition to other protection, protection against any and all claims, demands, and causes of action arising out of any act, omission, negligence or otherwise giving rise to a third party claim. The amount of coverage shall be a minimum of three million dollars ($3,000,000) combined single limit, with no deductible amount for each single occurrence for bodily injury and/or property damage. HERC shall provide for ten (10) days notice to THC and HSI in the event of any modification, cancellation or termination. HERC agrees to furnish THC and HSI Certificates of Insurance evidencing same within thirty (30) days after the execution of this Agreement. In no event shall HERC perform or promote the carry out the activities contemplated under this Agreement or the Trademark, Trade Name, Domain and Related Rights License Agreement prior to receipt by THC and HSI of evidence of insurance.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Insurance. HERC shall, throughout the term of this Agreement, obtain and maintain at its own cost and expense, from a insurance company of their choosing, a liability insurance policy. The amount of coverage shall be determined by HERC's discretion for each single occurrence for bodily injury and/or property damage. HERC shall provide notice to THC and HSI in the event of any modification, cancellation or termination at their earliest convenience. HERC agrees to furnish THC and HSI Certificates of Insurance evidencing same at their discretion. In no event shall HERC perform or promote the carry out the activities contemplated under this Agreement or the Trademark, Trade Name, Domain and Related Rights License Agreement prior to receipt by THC and HSI of evidence of insurance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'AAA-rated', 'business interruption policy', 'advertising injury', and 'acceptable to THC and HSI' it introduces ambiguity and weakens the insurance requirements. This shift in responsibility increases the risk for THC and HSI because the insurance requirements are much more lenient. This could lead to non-compliance with basic contract principles because it is not specific enough.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the insurance obligations vague, the contract potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Under contract law, parties must act honestly and reasonably. The changes allow HERC to obtain minimal insurance coverage, impacting the protection afforded to THC and HSI. This directly contradicts the intention of the contract and the good faith standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the insurance requirements, increasing risk for THC and HSI, and potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original text mandated 'AAA-rated' insurance from a company 'acceptable to THC and HSI,' ensuring a reliable and mutually agreed-upon level of coverage. The revised text removes these specific requirements, granting HERC discretion over the insurance company and coverage amount. This directly affects the protection afforded to THC and HSI. The Law Snippet indicates that 'good faith' encompasses 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By allowing HERC to potentially obtain minimal insurance, the revised contract arguably deviates from these standards, suggesting a possible breach of the duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Indemnification of HERC related to use of HERTZ trademark during the Interim Period. HSI shall defend, indemnify and hold HERC harmless against any and all claims, suits, actions or other proceedings whatsoever brought against HERC based on third-party claims of trademark infringement in connection with HERC'S use of the HSI (HERTZ) Foreign ERB Trademarks, the HSI (HERTZ) US ERB Trademarks, the HERC (HERTZ) US ERB Trademarks and the Other Intellectual Property to the extent such claims, suits, actions or other proceedings are based upon use of the HERTZ element comprising a HSI (HERTZ) Foreign ERB Trademark, HSI (HERTZ) US ERB Trademark, HERC (HERTZ) US ERB Trademark or Other Intellectual Property during the Interim Period only and from claims of third parties against HERC or any of its affiliates stemming from HERTZ's use of the HERTZ trademarks.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Indemnification of HERC related to use of HERTZ trademark during the Interim Period. HSI shall potentially defend, indemnify and hold HERC harmless against claims, suits, actions or other proceedings brought against HERC based on third-party claims of trademark infringement in connection with HERC'S use of the HSI (HERTZ) Foreign ERB Trademarks, the HSI (HERTZ) US ERB Trademarks, the HERC (HERTZ) US ERB Trademarks and the Other Intellectual Property to the extent such claims, suits, actions or other proceedings are based upon use of the HERTZ element comprising a HSI (HERTZ) Foreign ERB Trademark, HSI (HERTZ) US ERB Trademark, HERC (HERTZ) US ERB Trademark or Other Intellectual Property during the Interim Period only and from claims of third parties against HERC or any of its affiliates stemming from HERTZ's use of the HERTZ trademarks. HSI's decision is final.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of "potentially" and "HSI's decision is final" makes the indemnification discretionary. This is a change of duties which is a critical element of this contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability of Indemnification Clauses
perturbation.law_explanation: The addition of 'potentially' undermines the enforceability of the indemnification clause, as Missouri law views indemnification clauses with scrutiny, especially if they are vague or ambiguous. By making indemnification discretionary, the modified text introduces ambiguity and undermines the clear contractual obligation required for indemnification agreements to be enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 434.100 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVIII CONTRACTS AND CONTRACTUAL RELATIONS Chapter 434 < > Effective - 28 Aug 1999 434.100. Construction contracts holding harmless a person's negligence or wrongdoing are void and contra to public policy, exceptions. — 1. Except as provided in subsection 2 of this section, in any contract or agreement for public or private construction work, a party's covenant, promise or agreement to indemnify or hold harmless another person from that person's own negligence or wrongdoing is void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable. 2. The provisions of subsection 1 of this section shall not apply to: (1) A party's covenant, promise or agreement to indemnify or hold harmless another person from the party's own negligence or wrongdoing or the negligence or wrongdoing of the party's subcontractors and suppliers of any tier; (2) A party's promise to cause another person or entity to be covered as an insured or additional insured in an insurance contract; (3) A contract or agreement between state agencies or political subdivisions or between such governmental agencies; (4) A contract or agreement between a private person and such governmental entities for the use or operation of public property or a public facility; (5) A contract or agreement with the owner of the public property for the construction, use, maintenance or operation of a private facility when it is located on such public property; (6) A permit, authorization or contract with such governmental entities for the movement of property on the public highways, roads or streets of this state or any political subdivision; (7) Construction bonds, or insurance contracts or agreements; (8) An agreement containing a party's promise to indemnify, defend or hold harmless another person, if the agreement also requires the party to obtain specifie
perturbation.justification: The added word "potentially" and the phrase "HSI's decision is final" in the changed text introduce a level of discretion that undermines the original indemnification obligation. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this change modifies a critical element of the contract. The Law Explanation states that discretionary language undermines the enforceability of the indemnification clause by introducing ambiguity. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RSMo Section 434.100, indicates that agreements holding harmless another person from their own negligence are void against public policy under certain circumstances. While the snippet doesn't directly address 'potentially' indemnifying, the Law Explanation suggests that ambiguity makes the agreement less enforceable, which is a related concern under Missouri law.
